title: Criminal Procedure
subject: Criminal Procedure
summary: Criminal Procedure covers Fourth Amendment searches and seizures, Fifth Amendment protections, Sixth Amendment rights, trial procedure, and post-trial review.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Criminal Procedure questions usually test whether government conduct violated a constitutional rule and, if so, what remedy follows.
The major constitutional sources are:
Fourth Amendment — searches, seizures, arrests, and the exclusionary rule
Fifth Amendment — self-incrimination, Miranda, and double jeopardy
Sixth Amendment — counsel, jury trial, confrontation, public trial, and speedy trial
Eighth Amendment — bail and cruel and unusual punishment
Fourteenth Amendment — incorporation and due process

sections.title: 1. Criminal Procedure Attack Plan
body: For every question, ask:
Which constitutional right is involved?
Has the right attached?
Did the defendant personally possess the right?
What government conduct occurred?
What level of justification was required?
Was there a warrant or a valid exception?
Was the right invoked or waived?
What evidence or proceeding resulted?
What remedy applies?
Does an exception prevent suppression or reversal?
Memory line: Right, attachment, violation, remedy, exception.

sections.title: 2. Fourth Amendment Framework
body: PART I — FOURTH AMENDMENT
The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable:
Searches
Seizures of persons
Seizures of property
When a warrant is required, it must be:
Issued by a neutral and detached magistrate
Supported by probable cause
Supported by oath or affirmation
Particular as to the place and items or persons

blocks.text: PART I — FOURTH AMENDMENT

Searches
Seizures of persons
Seizures of property

Supported by probable cause
Supported by oath or affirmation
Particular as to the place and items or persons

sections.title: 3. Personal Rights and Standing
body: Fourth Amendment rights are personal.
A defendant may challenge government conduct only if the defendant:
Was personally seized, or
Had a reasonable expectation of privacy in the place searched or item seized, or
Suffered a physical intrusion on the defendant’s protected person, house, papers, or effects
Possession of incriminating evidence alone does not create standing.
Memory line: Evidence against you is not enough; the government must violate your own rights.

sections.title: 4. Government Action
body: The Fourth Amendment restricts government conduct.
A private search does not trigger the Fourth Amendment unless the private person acts as an instrument or agent of the government.
Relevant factors include whether:
Police encouraged or directed the search
Police knew about and acquiesced in it
The private person intended to assist law enforcement

sections.title: 5. The Exclusionary Rule
body: The exclusionary rule generally prevents the prosecution from using unlawfully obtained evidence in its case in chief.
It ordinarily does not apply to:
Grand jury proceedings
Civil proceedings
Parole-revocation hearings
Sentencing
Federal habeas review of state convictions
Impeachment of the defendant with voluntary statements
The rule is designed to deter police misconduct.

sections.title: 6. What Is a Seizure of a Person?
body: A person is seized when:
An officer intentionally applies physical force with intent to restrain, even if the person escapes, or
The person submits to a show of police authority
When there is no physical force, ask whether a reasonable person would feel free to leave or refuse the officer’s request.

sections.title: 7. Show of Authority Without Submission
body: A show of authority alone is not a seizure unless the person submits.

Example: Officers activate lights and order a cyclist to stop. The cyclist continues riding and discards contraband before stopping. The seizure occurs only when the cyclist submits, so the discarded item may be considered abandoned.

sections.title: 8. Accidental Force
body: Accidental government force is not a Fourth Amendment seizure.
The officer must intentionally use force or authority to restrain.
A negligent collision with a suspect is analyzed under due process, not as a Fourth Amendment seizure.

blocks.text: Accidental government force is not a Fourth Amendment seizure.

sections.title: 9. Arrest Warrants
body: An arrest warrant requires probable cause that:
A crime occurred
The named person committed it
An arrest warrant permits entry into the arrestee’s own home when officers reasonably believe the arrestee is inside.

blocks.text: An arrest warrant requires probable cause that:

The named person committed it
An arrest warrant permits entry into the arrestee’s own home when officers reasonably believe the arrestee is inside.

sections.title: 10. Arrest in the Arrestee’s Home
body: Police generally need:
An arrest warrant, or
Consent, or
Exigent circumstances
to enter a person’s home to make an arrest.
An arrest warrant alone generally authorizes entry into the arrestee’s home, but not a full evidentiary search.

blocks.text: Police generally need:
An arrest warrant, or
Consent, or
Exigent circumstances
to enter a person’s home to make an arrest.
An arrest warrant alone generally authorizes entry into the arrestee’s home, but not a full evidentiary search.

sections.title: 11. Arrest in a Third Party’s Home
body: To enter a third party’s home looking for the subject of an arrest warrant, police generally need:
A search warrant for the third party’s home
Consent
Exigent circumstances
The third party may challenge the unlawful home search.
The arrestee usually cannot challenge the search merely because the arrest occurred there.

sections.title: 12. Public Arrests
body: Police generally may make a warrantless arrest in public when they have probable cause.
They do not need to obtain an arrest warrant merely because time permits.

blocks.text: Police generally may make a warrantless arrest in public when they have probable cause.
They do not need to obtain an arrest warrant merely because time permits.

sections.title: 13. Warrantless Felony Arrest
body: Police may arrest without a warrant when they have probable cause to believe the person committed a felony.
A private citizen traditionally may arrest only when:
A felony actually occurred
The citizen reasonably believes the arrested person committed it

blocks.text: Police may arrest without a warrant when they have probable cause to believe the person committed a felony.

The citizen reasonably believes the arrested person committed it

sections.title: 14. Warrantless Misdemeanor Arrest
body: At common law, the misdemeanor generally must occur in the arresting person’s presence.
The Fourth Amendment permits a custodial arrest even for a fine-only offense when probable cause and applicable arrest authority exist.

blocks.text: At common law, the misdemeanor generally must occur in the arresting person’s presence.
The Fourth Amendment permits a custodial arrest even for a fine-only offense when probable cause and applicable arrest authority exist.

sections.title: 15. Effect of an Illegal Arrest
body: An illegal arrest does not itself prevent prosecution.
However, evidence obtained because of the illegal arrest may be suppressed.
A later confession is analyzed for:
Voluntariness
Miranda compliance
Attenuation from the unlawful arrest

blocks.text: An illegal arrest does not itself prevent prosecution.

sections.title: 16. What Is a Search?
body: A search occurs when the government:
Violates a reasonable expectation of privacy, or
Physically intrudes on a protected person, house, paper, or effect to obtain information
The second theory is sometimes called the property-based or trespass approach.

blocks.text: A search occurs when the government:

The second theory is sometimes called the property-based or trespass approach.

sections.title: 17. Reasonable Expectation of Privacy
body: Ask:
Did the person actually expect privacy?
Is that expectation one society recognizes as reasonable?
A defendant must establish both.

blocks.text: Ask:
Did the person actually expect privacy?
Is that expectation one society recognizes as reasonable?
A defendant must establish both.

sections.title: 18. The Home
body: The home receives the highest Fourth Amendment protection.
Protection generally includes:
Owners
Tenants
Dormitory occupants
Overnight guests
A person temporarily present solely for a short illegal business transaction may lack a reasonable expectation of privacy.

blocks.text: The home receives the highest Fourth Amendment protection.

Dormitory occupants
Overnight guests
A person temporarily present solely for a short illegal business transaction may lack a reasonable expectation of privacy.

sections.title: 19. Curtilage
body: Curtilage is the area immediately associated with domestic life.
Consider:
Proximity to the home
Inclusion within the home’s enclosure
Domestic use
Steps taken to shield the area from observation
Memory line: Near, enclosed, domestic, protected.

sections.title: 20. Open Fields
body: Land outside the curtilage is not protected by the open-fields doctrine.
This remains true even if the land is:
Privately owned
Fenced
Posted with “No Trespassing” signs
Hidden from public view

blocks.text: Land outside the curtilage is not protected by the open-fields doctrine.

Posted with “No Trespassing” signs

sections.title: 21. Hotel and Motel Rooms
body: A registered guest ordinarily has a reasonable expectation of privacy during the rental period.
A clerk cannot consent to a police search while the room remains validly occupied.
Privacy generally ends after checkout or lawful eviction.

blocks.text: A registered guest ordinarily has a reasonable expectation of privacy during the rental period.

Privacy generally ends after checkout or lawful eviction.

sections.title: 22. Businesses and Prisons
body: Business premises receive Fourth Amendment protection, subject to administrative-search rules.
A convicted prisoner has no reasonable expectation of privacy in a prison cell.
Pretrial detainees have limited privacy subject to institutional security needs.

blocks.text: Business premises receive Fourth Amendment protection, subject to administrative-search rules.

Pretrial detainees have limited privacy subject to institutional security needs.

sections.title: 23. Papers, Luggage, and Containers
body: People generally have privacy in:
Closed luggage
Sealed containers
Personal papers
Digital devices
Exploratory physical manipulation of luggage may be a search.
Odors exposed to the public generally are not protected.

blocks.text: People generally have privacy in:

Odors exposed to the public generally are not protected.

sections.title: 24. Third-Party Doctrine
body: A person traditionally assumes the risk that information voluntarily given to a third party will be disclosed to government.
Examples include some:
Bank records
Business records
Information shared with informants
However, collection of extensive historical cell-site location information generally requires a warrant.

sections.title: 25. Automobiles
body: There is a reduced expectation of privacy in automobiles because they are mobile and heavily regulated.
A lawful possessor may have privacy even if not listed on a rental agreement.
A stop of a vehicle seizes:
The driver
Every passenger

sections.title: 26. Checkpoints
body: Suspicionless vehicle checkpoints may be valid when:
Neutral standards govern the stops
The purpose is closely related to roadway safety or border control
The intrusion is limited
Examples usually permitted:
Sobriety checkpoints
Fixed immigration checkpoints
Checkpoints seeking public assistance about a recent crime
A checkpoint whose primary purpose is ordinary drug interdiction is generally invalid.

sections.title: 27. Abandoned Property
body: There is no reasonable expectation of privacy in abandoned property.
Examples may include:
Garbage left for curbside collection
Property intentionally discarded during flight before seizure
Items clearly relinquished
Abandonment must be voluntary and judged objectively.

sections.title: 28. Physical Characteristics
body: The Fifth Amendment does not protect physical characteristics, and a person generally lacks privacy in visible or identifying traits.
Police may often compel:
Voice samples
Handwriting samples
Fingerprints
Lineup participation
Taking blood, urine, hair, or DNA is a search and must be reasonable under the Fourth Amendment.

sections.title: 29. DNA Booking Searches
body: After a lawful arrest supported by probable cause for a serious offense, a cheek swab may be a reasonable routine booking procedure.
The government may retain and compare the DNA profile.

blocks.text: After a lawful arrest supported by probable cause for a serious offense, a cheek swab may be a reasonable routine booking procedure.
The government may retain and compare the DNA profile.

sections.title: 30. Blood and Breath Tests
body: A blood draw is a significant search and usually requires a warrant unless an exception applies.
A breath test incident to a lawful drunk-driving arrest generally may be required without a warrant.
Natural dissipation of alcohol does not automatically create an exigency for every blood draw.

blocks.text: A blood draw is a significant search and usually requires a warrant unless an exception applies.

Natural dissipation of alcohol does not automatically create an exigency for every blood draw.

sections.title: 31. Informants and Recorded Conversations
body: A speaker assumes the risk that the listener is:
An informant
An undercover officer
Recording the conversation
Transmitting it to police
One-party consent to monitoring generally defeats the other speaker’s Fourth Amendment claim.

blocks.text: A speaker assumes the risk that the listener is:

An undercover officer
Recording the conversation
Transmitting it to police
One-party consent to monitoring generally defeats the other speaker’s Fourth Amendment claim.

sections.title: 32. Aerial Observation
body: Observation from lawful public airspace generally is not a search when the public could lawfully observe from that altitude.
Physical intrusion or unusually invasive surveillance may change the result.

blocks.text: Observation from lawful public airspace generally is not a search when the public could lawfully observe from that altitude.
Physical intrusion or unusually invasive surveillance may change the result.

sections.title: 33. Technology and Tracking
body: A search may occur when police:
Physically attach a GPS tracker to a suspect’s property
Attach a tracking device to a person
Obtain extensive historical cell-site location data
Use uncommon sense-enhancing technology to learn details inside a home
Ordinary tools such as flashlights generally do not create a search.

blocks.text: A search may occur when police:

Attach a tracking device to a person
Obtain extensive historical cell-site location data
Use uncommon sense-enhancing technology to learn details inside a home
Ordinary tools such as flashlights generally do not create a search.

sections.title: 34. Thermal Imaging
body: Using technology not in general public use to obtain information about the interior of a home that otherwise would require physical intrusion is a search.
Memory line: Advanced technology aimed inside the home usually requires a warrant.

blocks.text: Using technology not in general public use to obtain information about the interior of a home that otherwise would require physical intrusion is a search.
Memory line: Advanced technology aimed inside the home usually requires a warrant.

sections.title: 35. Dog Sniffs
body: A dog sniff in a lawful public setting generally is not a search because it reveals only the presence or absence of contraband.
But bringing a drug-sniffing dog onto a home’s porch or curtilage to investigate the home is a search.
A dog sniff may not prolong a completed traffic stop without reasonable suspicion.

sections.title: 36. Search Warrants
body: A valid search warrant requires:
Neutral and detached magistrate
Probable cause
Oath or affirmation
Particularity
Proper execution

blocks.text: A valid search warrant requires:
Neutral and detached magistrate
Probable cause
Oath or affirmation
Particularity
Proper execution

sections.title: 37. Probable Cause to Search
body: Probable cause exists when the totality of circumstances creates a fair probability that evidence or contraband will be found in the place searched.
Probable cause is more than reasonable suspicion but less than certainty.

blocks.text: Probable cause exists when the totality of circumstances creates a fair probability that evidence or contraband will be found in the place searched.
Probable cause is more than reasonable suspicion but less than certainty.

sections.title: 38. Informant Tips
body: Informant reliability is evaluated under the totality of circumstances.
Relevant factors include:
Past reliability
Basis of knowledge
Detail
Predictive information
Police corroboration
Statement against interest
No single factor is mandatory.

blocks.text: Informant reliability is evaluated under the totality of circumstances.

Detail
Predictive information
Police corroboration
Statement against interest
No single factor is mandatory.

sections.title: 39. Franks Challenge
body: A defendant may challenge a facially valid warrant affidavit by showing:
The affiant knowingly, intentionally, or recklessly included a false statement or material omission
The falsehood or omission was necessary to probable cause
If corrected probable cause remains, suppression is denied.

blocks.text: A defendant may challenge a facially valid warrant affidavit by showing:

sections.title: 40. Particularity
body: A warrant must particularly describe:
The place to search
The persons or things to seize
A supporting affidavit may cure ambiguity only when properly incorporated and accompanying the warrant.
A warrant cannot authorize a general exploratory search.

blocks.text: A warrant must particularly describe:
The place to search
The persons or things to seize
A supporting affidavit may cure ambiguity only when properly incorporated and accompanying the warrant.
A warrant cannot authorize a general exploratory search.

sections.title: 41. Anticipatory Warrants
body: An anticipatory warrant is valid when probable cause exists to believe:
A triggering condition will occur
Once it occurs, evidence will be at the location

blocks.text: An anticipatory warrant is valid when probable cause exists to believe:
A triggering condition will occur
Once it occurs, evidence will be at the location

sections.title: 42. Execution of Warrants
body: A warrant generally must be:
Executed by authorized government officers
Executed while probable cause remains fresh
Limited to places where the listed items could be found
Ended once the authorized search is complete

blocks.text: A warrant generally must be:

Executed while probable cause remains fresh
Limited to places where the listed items could be found
Ended once the authorized search is complete

sections.title: 43. Knock and Announce
body: Police ordinarily must announce identity and purpose before forcible entry.
Exceptions may apply when officers reasonably fear:
Violence
Escape
Destruction of evidence
Violation of knock-and-announce generally does not require suppression when the search was otherwise valid.

blocks.text: Police ordinarily must announce identity and purpose before forcible entry.

sections.title: 44. Persons Present During a Search
body: A premises warrant does not automatically authorize searching everyone present.
Police need independent justification to search an unnamed person.
However, occupants in the immediate vicinity may be reasonably detained during execution of a valid search warrant.

blocks.text: A premises warrant does not automatically authorize searching everyone present.

sections.title: 45. Warrant Exceptions Overview
body: Use ESCAPIST:
Exigent circumstances
Search incident to arrest
Consent
Automobile
Plain view
Inventory and administrative searches
Stop and frisk
Temporary special-needs and border searches

blocks.text: Use ESCAPIST:

Search incident to arrest
Consent
Automobile
Plain view
Inventory and administrative searches
Stop and frisk
Temporary special-needs and border searches

sections.title: 46. Search Incident to Lawful Arrest
body: After a lawful custodial arrest, police may search:
The arrestee’s person
Clothing
Containers immediately associated with the person
The area within immediate control
The search must be substantially contemporaneous with the arrest.
A citation without arrest does not justify a full search incident to arrest.

blocks.text: After a lawful custodial arrest, police may search:
The arrestee’s person
Clothing
Containers immediately associated with the person
The area within immediate control
The search must be substantially contemporaneous with the arrest.
A citation without arrest does not justify a full search incident to arrest.

sections.title: 47. Chimel Wingspan
body: Police may search the area from which the arrestee might obtain:
A weapon
Destructible evidence
Once the arrestee cannot access an area, other exceptions must justify searching it.

blocks.text: Police may search the area from which the arrestee might obtain:
A weapon
Destructible evidence
Once the arrestee cannot access an area, other exceptions must justify searching it.

sections.title: 48. Cell Phones Incident to Arrest
body: Police generally may seize a cell phone but need a warrant to search its digital contents.
Exigent circumstances may justify immediate access in an emergency.

blocks.text: Police generally may seize a cell phone but need a warrant to search its digital contents.
Exigent circumstances may justify immediate access in an emergency.

sections.title: 49. Protective Sweep
body: During an in-home arrest, officers may inspect immediately adjoining spaces where an attacker could hide.
A broader sweep requires reasonable suspicion that a dangerous person is present.
A protective sweep is limited to spaces large enough to conceal a person.

blocks.text: During an in-home arrest, officers may inspect immediately adjoining spaces where an attacker could hide.
A broader sweep requires reasonable suspicion that a dangerous person is present.
A protective sweep is limited to spaces large enough to conceal a person.

sections.title: 50. Vehicle Search Incident to Arrest
body: Police may search the passenger compartment incident to arrest only when:
The arrestee can access the passenger compartment at the time of the search, or
It is reasonable to believe evidence of the offense of arrest is inside
Memory line: Access or arrest-offense evidence.

blocks.text: Police may search the passenger compartment incident to arrest only when:
The arrestee can access the passenger compartment at the time of the search, or
It is reasonable to believe evidence of the offense of arrest is inside
Memory line: Access or arrest-offense evidence.

sections.title: 51. Exigent Circumstances
body: Warrantless entry may be justified by probable cause plus an emergency such as:
Hot pursuit
Immediate threat to safety
Imminent destruction of evidence
Likely escape
Emergency aid
The scope must match the emergency.

blocks.text: Warrantless entry may be justified by probable cause plus an emergency such as:
Hot pursuit
Immediate threat to safety
Imminent destruction of evidence
Likely escape
Emergency aid
The scope must match the emergency.

sections.title: 52. Police-Created Exigency
body: Police may rely on an exigency unless they created it by engaging in or threatening conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment.
Lawful knocking and announcing does not improperly create an exigency merely because occupants begin destroying evidence.

blocks.text: Police may rely on an exigency unless they created it by engaging in or threatening conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment.
Lawful knocking and announcing does not improperly create an exigency merely because occupants begin destroying evidence.

sections.title: 53. Hot Pursuit
body: Hot pursuit of a fleeing felon generally permits entry into a home.
Pursuit of a misdemeanor suspect does not automatically justify home entry; courts examine the totality of circumstances and seriousness of the offense.
A minor nonjailable offense rarely supports warrantless home entry.

blocks.text: Hot pursuit of a fleeing felon generally permits entry into a home.

A minor nonjailable offense rarely supports warrantless home entry.

sections.title: 54. Emergency Aid
body: Police may enter when objectively reasonable facts indicate an occupant needs immediate aid or protection.
The officer’s subjective investigative motive is not controlling.

blocks.text: Police may enter when objectively reasonable facts indicate an occupant needs immediate aid or protection.
The officer’s subjective investigative motive is not controlling.

sections.title: 55. Terry Stop
body: A Terry stop is a brief investigative detention supported by reasonable suspicion that criminal activity is occurring or has occurred.
Reasonable suspicion requires:
Specific and articulable facts
Rational inferences
More than a hunch
Less than probable cause

blocks.text: A Terry stop is a brief investigative detention supported by reasonable suspicion that criminal activity is occurring or has occurred.
Reasonable suspicion requires:
Specific and articulable facts
Rational inferences
More than a hunch
Less than probable cause

sections.title: 56. Informant Tips and Reasonable Suspicion
body: An anonymous tip may support a stop when sufficiently reliable through:
Detailed description
Predictive information
Contemporaneous reporting
Use of emergency systems such as 911
Police corroboration

blocks.text: An anonymous tip may support a stop when sufficiently reliable through:
Detailed description
Predictive information
Contemporaneous reporting
Use of emergency systems such as 911
Police corroboration

sections.title: 57. Flight and High-Crime Areas
body: Presence in a high-crime area alone is insufficient.
Unprovoked flight upon seeing police, combined with surrounding circumstances, may create reasonable suspicion.

blocks.text: Presence in a high-crime area alone is insufficient.
Unprovoked flight upon seeing police, combined with surrounding circumstances, may create reasonable suspicion.

sections.title: 58. Reasonable Mistake of Law
body: A reasonable police mistake about an ambiguous law may support reasonable suspicion.
An unreasonable legal mistake cannot.

blocks.text: A reasonable police mistake about an ambiguous law may support reasonable suspicion.
An unreasonable legal mistake cannot.

sections.title: 59. Terry Frisk
body: A frisk requires reasonable suspicion that the stopped person is armed and dangerous.
The frisk is a limited pat-down for weapons, not a general evidence search.

blocks.text: A frisk requires reasonable suspicion that the stopped person is armed and dangerous.
The frisk is a limited pat-down for weapons, not a general evidence search.

sections.title: 60. Plain Feel
body: During a lawful frisk, police may seize contraband when its identity is immediately apparent through lawful touch.
Officers may not manipulate the object to determine what it is.

blocks.text: During a lawful frisk, police may seize contraband when its identity is immediately apparent through lawful touch.
Officers may not manipulate the object to determine what it is.

sections.title: 61. Vehicle Protective Search
body: During a lawful stop, police may search areas of the passenger compartment where weapons could be hidden if they reasonably believe the suspect is dangerous and may gain access to weapons.
This is different from a vehicle search incident to arrest.

blocks.text: During a lawful stop, police may search areas of the passenger compartment where weapons could be hidden if they reasonably believe the suspect is dangerous and may gain access to weapons.
This is different from a vehicle search incident to arrest.

sections.title: 62. Duration of a Terry Stop
body: A stop must last no longer than reasonably necessary to investigate the suspected offense.
Police must use reasonably efficient methods.
If detention becomes too long or intrusive, it becomes an arrest requiring probable cause.

blocks.text: A stop must last no longer than reasonably necessary to investigate the suspected offense.
Police must use reasonably efficient methods.
If detention becomes too long or intrusive, it becomes an arrest requiring probable cause.

sections.title: 63. Automobile Exception
body: Police may search a vehicle without a warrant when they have probable cause that it contains evidence or contraband.
They may search every location and container capable of holding the object sought.
The vehicle need not be searched immediately.

blocks.text: Police may search a vehicle without a warrant when they have probable cause that it contains evidence or contraband.
They may search every location and container capable of holding the object sought.
The vehicle need not be searched immediately.

sections.title: 64. Scope of Automobile Probable Cause
body: If probable cause concerns the entire vehicle, police may search the entire vehicle, including the trunk and locked containers.
If probable cause concerns only one specific container, police may search that container but not unrelated areas without additional probable cause.

blocks.text: If probable cause concerns the entire vehicle, police may search the entire vehicle, including the trunk and locked containers.
If probable cause concerns only one specific container, police may search that container but not unrelated areas without additional probable cause.

sections.title: 65. Passenger Belongings
body: The automobile exception permits searching a passenger’s belongings if the container could hold the object for which police have vehicle-wide probable cause.

blocks.text: The automobile exception permits searching a passenger’s belongings if the container could hold the object for which police have vehicle-wide probable cause.

sections.title: 66. Automobile Exception and Curtilage
body: The automobile exception does not permit officers to enter a home or its curtilage to reach a vehicle.
Police need another lawful basis for the entry.

blocks.text: The automobile exception does not permit officers to enter a home or its curtilage to reach a vehicle.
Police need another lawful basis for the entry.

sections.title: 67. Pretextual Stops
body: A traffic stop is valid when officers objectively have probable cause or reasonable suspicion of a traffic violation, even if their real motive is to investigate another crime.

blocks.text: A traffic stop is valid when officers objectively have probable cause or reasonable suspicion of a traffic violation, even if their real motive is to investigate another crime.

sections.title: 68. Plain View
body: Police may seize an item when:
They are lawfully present
They have lawful access to the item
Its incriminating nature is immediately apparent
Discovery need not be inadvertent.

blocks.text: Police may seize an item when:
They are lawfully present
They have lawful access to the item
Its incriminating nature is immediately apparent
Discovery need not be inadvertent.

sections.title: 69. Consent Searches
body: Valid consent must be voluntary under the totality of circumstances.
Police need not tell the person that consent may be refused.
Consent obtained through a false claim of a warrant is invalid.

blocks.text: Valid consent must be voluntary under the totality of circumstances.
Police need not tell the person that consent may be refused.
Consent obtained through a false claim of a warrant is invalid.

sections.title: 70. Scope of Consent
body: The search may extend only as far as a reasonable officer would understand the consent.
Consent to search a car for small drugs generally includes containers capable of holding them.
The person may limit or withdraw consent.

blocks.text: The search may extend only as far as a reasonable officer would understand the consent.
Consent to search a car for small drugs generally includes containers capable of holding them.
The person may limit or withdraw consent.

sections.title: 71. Third-Party Consent
body: A third party may consent when the person has:
Actual common authority, or
Apparent authority reasonably believed by police
Common authority depends on joint access or control, not title alone.

blocks.text: A third party may consent when the person has:
Actual common authority, or
Apparent authority reasonably believed by police
Common authority depends on joint access or control, not title alone.

sections.title: 72. Present Objecting Occupant
body: When two occupants are physically present and one expressly refuses consent, police generally may not rely on the other occupant’s consent.
If the objector is lawfully removed for reasons unrelated to avoiding the objection, the remaining occupant may later consent.

blocks.text: When two occupants are physically present and one expressly refuses consent, police generally may not rely on the other occupant’s consent.
If the objector is lawfully removed for reasons unrelated to avoiding the objection, the remaining occupant may later consent.

sections.title: 73. Landlords, Hotel Staff, and Parents
body: A landlord ordinarily cannot consent to a search of a tenant’s private apartment.
Hotel staff cannot consent during a valid rental period.
A parent may often consent to a search of a child’s room, but authority over a locked private container may be absent.

blocks.text: A landlord ordinarily cannot consent to a search of a tenant’s private apartment.
Hotel staff cannot consent during a valid rental period.
A parent may often consent to a search of a child’s room, but authority over a locked private container may be absent.

sections.title: 74. Administrative Searches
body: Administrative warrants may issue under a lower probable-cause standard based on:
A reasonable regulatory plan
Area-wide inspection criteria
Evidence of a regulatory violation
Administrative searches cannot be used as a pretext for ordinary criminal investigation.

blocks.text: Administrative warrants may issue under a lower probable-cause standard based on:
A reasonable regulatory plan
Area-wide inspection criteria
Evidence of a regulatory violation
Administrative searches cannot be used as a pretext for ordinary criminal investigation.

sections.title: 75. Closely Regulated Industries
body: Warrantless inspections may be valid in closely regulated industries when the regulatory scheme:
Serves a substantial government interest
Is necessary to the regulatory scheme
Provides a constitutionally adequate substitute for a warrant

blocks.text: Warrantless inspections may be valid in closely regulated industries when the regulatory scheme:
Serves a substantial government interest
Is necessary to the regulatory scheme
Provides a constitutionally adequate substitute for a warrant

sections.title: 76. School Searches
body: Public school officials may search a student when:
There is reasonable suspicion
The search is reasonably related to its objective
The search is not excessively intrusive considering age, sex, and suspected offense

blocks.text: Public school officials may search a student when:
There is reasonable suspicion
The search is reasonably related to its objective
The search is not excessively intrusive considering age, sex, and suspected offense

sections.title: 77. Special-Needs Searches
body: A suspicionless search may be valid when a real special need beyond ordinary law enforcement outweighs privacy interests.
Examples may include:
Safety-sensitive employee drug testing
Student-athlete testing
Certain probation and parole searches

blocks.text: A suspicionless search may be valid when a real special need beyond ordinary law enforcement outweighs privacy interests.
Examples may include:
Safety-sensitive employee drug testing
Student-athlete testing
Certain probation and parole searches

sections.title: 78. Inventory Searches
body: Police may inventory lawfully impounded property to:
Protect property
Protect police from false claims
Protect officers and the public
The search must follow standardized procedures and cannot be a pretextual investigatory rummage.

blocks.text: Police may inventory lawfully impounded property to:
Protect property
Protect police from false claims
Protect officers and the public
The search must follow standardized procedures and cannot be a pretextual investigatory rummage.

sections.title: 79. Border Searches
body: Routine searches at the international border or its functional equivalent generally require no warrant or individualized suspicion.
Highly intrusive or nonroutine searches may require reasonable suspicion.

blocks.text: Routine searches at the international border or its functional equivalent generally require no warrant or individualized suspicion.
Highly intrusive or nonroutine searches may require reasonable suspicion.

sections.title: 80. Wiretaps
body: A wiretap warrant generally must:
Identify the suspected offense
Identify persons when known
Describe the communications
Be limited in duration
Include minimization and termination requirements
Rest on probable cause
One-party consensual monitoring does not require a wiretap warrant.

blocks.text: A wiretap warrant generally must:

Identify persons when known
Describe the communications
Be limited in duration
Include minimization and termination requirements
Rest on probable cause
One-party consensual monitoring does not require a wiretap warrant.

sections.title: 81. Fruit of the Poisonous Tree
body: The exclusionary rule may suppress:
Directly seized evidence
Derivative physical evidence
Later statements
Witnesses discovered through illegality
The defendant must show a causal connection to the constitutional violation.

blocks.text: The exclusionary rule may suppress:
Directly seized evidence
Derivative physical evidence
Later statements
Witnesses discovered through illegality
The defendant must show a causal connection to the constitutional violation.

sections.title: 82. Inevitable Discovery
body: Evidence is admissible when the prosecution proves it would inevitably have been discovered through lawful means.
The lawful discovery process must be more than speculation.

blocks.text: Evidence is admissible when the prosecution proves it would inevitably have been discovered through lawful means.
The lawful discovery process must be more than speculation.

sections.title: 83. Independent Source
body: Evidence is admissible when obtained through a genuinely independent lawful source unconnected to the illegality.

blocks.text: Evidence is admissible when obtained through a genuinely independent lawful source unconnected to the illegality.

sections.title: 84. Attenuation
body: Evidence is admissible when the connection to the illegality becomes sufficiently remote.
Consider:
Time elapsed
Intervening circumstances
Purpose and flagrancy of police misconduct
Discovery of a valid outstanding arrest warrant may attenuate an unlawful stop.

blocks.text: Evidence is admissible when the connection to the illegality becomes sufficiently remote.
Consider:
Time elapsed
Intervening circumstances
Purpose and flagrancy of police misconduct
Discovery of a valid outstanding arrest warrant may attenuate an unlawful stop.

sections.title: 85. Good-Faith Exception
body: Evidence may be admitted when police objectively and reasonably rely on:
A facially valid warrant later invalidated
Binding law later overruled
Erroneous database information caused by isolated negligence

blocks.text: Evidence may be admitted when police objectively and reasonably rely on:
A facially valid warrant later invalidated
Binding law later overruled
Erroneous database information caused by isolated negligence

sections.title: 86. Limits on Good Faith
body: Good faith does not apply when:
The affidavit is so lacking in probable cause that reliance is unreasonable
The warrant is facially defective
The affiant knowingly or recklessly misled the magistrate
The magistrate abandoned neutrality
Police improperly execute the warrant

blocks.text: Good faith does not apply when:

The affiant knowingly or recklessly misled the magistrate
The magistrate abandoned neutrality
Police improperly execute the warrant

sections.title: 87. Impeachment Use
body: Voluntary statements obtained in violation of Miranda may be used to impeach the defendant.
Illegally obtained physical evidence may sometimes be used for impeachment depending on the constitutional violation.
Coerced statements may never be used, even for impeachment.

blocks.text: Voluntary statements obtained in violation of Miranda may be used to impeach the defendant.
Illegally obtained physical evidence may sometimes be used for impeachment depending on the constitutional violation.
Coerced statements may never be used, even for impeachment.

sections.title: 88. Privilege Against Self-Incrimination
body: PART II — FIFTH AMENDMENT
The Fifth Amendment protects a natural person from being compelled to provide testimonial evidence that may incriminate that person.
Use CTI:
Compelled
Testimonial
Incriminating

blocks.text: PART II — FIFTH AMENDMENT
The Fifth Amendment protects a natural person from being compelled to provide testimonial evidence that may incriminate that person.
Use CTI:
Compelled
Testimonial
Incriminating

sections.title: 89. Persons Protected
body: The privilege belongs to natural persons.
It generally does not protect:
Corporations
Partnerships
Labor unions
Custodians of organizational records
A sole proprietor may invoke the privilege in some circumstances.

blocks.text: The privilege belongs to natural persons.
It generally does not protect:
Corporations
Partnerships
Labor unions
Custodians of organizational records
A sole proprietor may invoke the privilege in some circumstances.

sections.title: 90. Testimonial vs. Physical Evidence
body: Protected testimonial evidence communicates factual assertions or reveals the contents of the mind.
Generally unprotected physical evidence includes:
Fingerprints
Voice exemplars
Handwriting exemplars
Blood samples
Breath tests
Lineup participation

blocks.text: Protected testimonial evidence communicates factual assertions or reveals the contents of the mind.
Generally unprotected physical evidence includes:
Fingerprints
Voice exemplars
Handwriting exemplars
Blood samples
Breath tests
Lineup participation

sections.title: 91. Act-of-Production Doctrine
body: Producing documents may itself be testimonial when production communicates:
The documents exist
The producer possesses or controls them
The documents are authentic
A valid search warrant may allow police to seize documents without compelling the owner to produce them.

blocks.text: Producing documents may itself be testimonial when production communicates:
The documents exist
The producer possesses or controls them
The documents are authentic
A valid search warrant may allow police to seize documents without compelling the owner to produce them.

sections.title: 92. Invoking the Privilege
body: A criminal defendant invokes the privilege by not testifying.
A witness generally must take the stand and assert the privilege question by question when an answer could reasonably incriminate the witness.

blocks.text: A criminal defendant invokes the privilege by not testifying.
A witness generally must take the stand and assert the privilege question by question when an answer could reasonably incriminate the witness.

sections.title: 93. Waiver of the Privilege
body: A defendant who testifies waives the privilege as to proper cross-examination within the scope of testimony and credibility.
A witness may waive the privilege by voluntarily revealing incriminating facts.

blocks.text: A defendant who testifies waives the privilege as to proper cross-examination within the scope of testimony and credibility.
A witness may waive the privilege by voluntarily revealing incriminating facts.

sections.title: 94. Immunity
body: The government may compel testimony by granting use and derivative-use immunity.
This bars:
Use of the testimony
Use of evidence derived from it
Transactional immunity is broader but not constitutionally required.

blocks.text: The government may compel testimony by granting use and derivative-use immunity.
This bars:
Use of the testimony
Use of evidence derived from it
Transactional immunity is broader but not constitutionally required.

sections.title: 95. Miranda Overview
body: Miranda applies only to:
Custody
Interrogation
By a known government agent
If any element is absent, warnings are not constitutionally required.
Memory line: Custody plus interrogation equals Miranda.

blocks.text: Miranda applies only to:
Custody
Interrogation
By a known government agent
If any element is absent, warnings are not constitutionally required.
Memory line: Custody plus interrogation equals Miranda.

sections.title: 96. Miranda Custody
body: Custody exists when:
There is formal arrest, or
A reasonable person would feel restrained to the degree associated with formal arrest
The test is objective under the totality of circumstances.
A child’s known or apparent age may be considered.

blocks.text: Custody exists when:
There is formal arrest, or
A reasonable person would feel restrained to the degree associated with formal arrest
The test is objective under the totality of circumstances.
A child’s known or apparent age may be considered.

sections.title: 97. Common Custody Settings
body: Usually custodial:
Formal arrest
Police-dominated stationhouse questioning with no freedom to leave
Usually not automatically custodial:
Ordinary traffic stop
Voluntary station interview
Brief roadside inquiry
Routine probation meeting
Prison questioning without added coercive restraints

blocks.text: Usually custodial:

Police-dominated stationhouse questioning with no freedom to leave
Usually not automatically custodial:

Voluntary station interview
Brief roadside inquiry
Routine probation meeting
Prison questioning without added coercive restraints

sections.title: 98. Miranda Interrogation
body: Interrogation includes:
Express questioning
Words or conduct police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response
Volunteered statements are not interrogation.

blocks.text: Interrogation includes:
Express questioning
Words or conduct police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response
Volunteered statements are not interrogation.

sections.title: 99. Voluntariness of Confessions
body: A confession violates due process only when police coercion overbears the defendant’s will.
Consider:
Threats
Promises
Length and conditions of questioning
Denial of basic needs
Age
Education
Mental condition
Intoxication
Mental illness alone is insufficient without coercive police activity.

blocks.text: A confession violates due process only when police coercion overbears the defendant’s will.
Consider:
Threats
Promises
Length and conditions of questioning
Denial of basic needs
Age
Education
Mental condition
Intoxication
Mental illness alone is insufficient without coercive police activity.

sections.title: 100. Miranda Warnings
body: Police must communicate:
Right to remain silent
Statements may be used in court
Right to an attorney during questioning
Appointed counsel if indigent
Exact wording is unnecessary if the substance is conveyed.

blocks.text: Police must communicate:
Right to remain silent
Statements may be used in court
Right to an attorney during questioning
Appointed counsel if indigent
Exact wording is unnecessary if the substance is conveyed.

sections.title: 101. Public-Safety Exception
body: Police may ask unwarned questions reasonably prompted by an immediate concern for public or officer safety.
The exception is limited to the emergency.

blocks.text: Police may ask unwarned questions reasonably prompted by an immediate concern for public or officer safety.
The exception is limited to the emergency.

sections.title: 102. Routine Booking and Undercover Exceptions
body: Miranda generally does not apply to:
Routine biographical booking questions
Questioning by an undercover agent whom the suspect does not know is a government agent
The coercive police-dominated atmosphere is missing in the undercover setting.

blocks.text: Miranda generally does not apply to:
Routine biographical booking questions
Questioning by an undercover agent whom the suspect does not know is a government agent
The coercive police-dominated atmosphere is missing in the undercover setting.

sections.title: 103. Miranda Waiver
body: The prosecution must prove a waiver was:
Knowing
Intelligent
Voluntary
A suspect who understands the warnings and answers questions without invoking may impliedly waive Miranda.
Mere silence does not itself invoke or waive.

blocks.text: The prosecution must prove a waiver was:

Intelligent
Voluntary
A suspect who understands the warnings and answers questions without invoking may impliedly waive Miranda.
Mere silence does not itself invoke or waive.

sections.title: 104. Invoking the Right to Counsel
body: The request for counsel must be unambiguous.
Once invoked during custodial interrogation:
Questioning must stop
Police may not initiate further custodial interrogation without counsel
The suspect may initiate communication
Police may reinitiate after a break in custody of at least 14 days with fresh warnings and waiver
This is the Edwards rule.

blocks.text: The request for counsel must be unambiguous.

Police may not initiate further custodial interrogation without counsel
The suspect may initiate communication
Police may reinitiate after a break in custody of at least 14 days with fresh warnings and waiver
This is the Edwards rule.

sections.title: 105. Invoking the Right to Silence
body: The invocation must be unambiguous.
Police must scrupulously honor it.
Later questioning may be permissible after:
Questioning stops
Significant time passes
Fresh warnings are given
The circumstances show respect for the earlier invocation

blocks.text: The invocation must be unambiguous.
Police must scrupulously honor it.

Significant time passes
Fresh warnings are given

sections.title: 106. Unwarned but Voluntary Statements
body: An unwarned voluntary statement generally:
Is inadmissible in the prosecution’s case in chief
May be used to impeach the defendant
Does not require suppression of physical fruits
A coerced statement cannot be used for any purpose.

blocks.text: An unwarned voluntary statement generally:

May be used to impeach the defendant
Does not require suppression of physical fruits
A coerced statement cannot be used for any purpose.

sections.title: 107. Two-Step Interrogation
body: A deliberate “question first, warn later” strategy may render a later warned confession inadmissible when the warnings do not realistically communicate a genuine choice.
Consider:
Continuity of personnel and location
Overlap in questions
Timing
Whether police treated the sessions as continuous
Whether curative measures were taken

sections.title: 108. Attachment of the Right to Counsel
body: PART III — SIXTH AMENDMENT COUNSEL
The Sixth Amendment right attaches when formal adversarial proceedings begin, such as:
Indictment
Information
Arraignment
Initial appearance before a judicial officer where charges are formally addressed
It does not attach merely because the person is a suspect.

blocks.text: PART III — SIXTH AMENDMENT COUNSEL

Information
Arraignment

sections.title: 109. Critical Stages
body: Counsel is required at critical stages where substantial rights may be affected, including:
Postcharge interrogation
Arraignment
Preliminary hearing
Plea bargaining and guilty plea
Trial
Sentencing
Postindictment corporeal lineup
Certain appeals as of right

sections.title: 110. Actual Imprisonment Rule
body: An indigent defendant has a right to appointed counsel when actual imprisonment is imposed.
A suspended sentence that may later result in incarceration also triggers the right.

blocks.text: An indigent defendant has a right to appointed counsel when actual imprisonment is imposed.
A suspended sentence that may later result in incarceration also triggers the right.

sections.title: 111. Deliberate Elicitation
body: After the Sixth Amendment attaches, government agents may not deliberately elicit statements about the charged offense without counsel or a valid waiver.
This is broader than express interrogation.
An undercover informant may violate the Sixth Amendment by deliberately eliciting information after attachment.

blocks.text: After the Sixth Amendment attaches, government agents may not deliberately elicit statements about the charged offense without counsel or a valid waiver.
This is broader than express interrogation.
An undercover informant may violate the Sixth Amendment by deliberately eliciting information after attachment.

sections.title: 112. Offense-Specific Nature
body: The Sixth Amendment is offense specific.
Police may question the defendant about an uncharged offense if:
Miranda is satisfied when applicable
The uncharged offense is not the same under the Blockburger elements test

blocks.text: The Sixth Amendment is offense specific.
Police may question the defendant about an uncharged offense if:
Miranda is satisfied when applicable
The uncharged offense is not the same under the Blockburger elements test

sections.title: 113. Sixth Amendment Waiver
body: A defendant may knowingly, intelligently, and voluntarily waive counsel.
Miranda warnings and waiver ordinarily suffice for postcharge custodial questioning.
Appointment of counsel alone does not prevent police from seeking a later waiver unless the defendant actually invoked the Miranda right to counsel.

blocks.text: A defendant may knowingly, intelligently, and voluntarily waive counsel.
Miranda warnings and waiver ordinarily suffice for postcharge custodial questioning.
Appointment of counsel alone does not prevent police from seeking a later waiver unless the defendant actually invoked the Miranda right to counsel.

sections.title: 114. Self-Representation
body: A competent defendant may waive counsel and proceed pro se when the waiver is knowing and intelligent.
The court should warn of the disadvantages.
A defendant competent to stand trial may still be found incompetent to conduct trial personally.
Standby counsel may be appointed.

blocks.text: A competent defendant may waive counsel and proceed pro se when the waiver is knowing and intelligent.
The court should warn of the disadvantages.

sections.title: 115. Ineffective Assistance of Counsel
body: Use Strickland:
Counsel’s performance was objectively deficient
The deficiency caused prejudice
Prejudice usually requires a reasonable probability that the result would have been different.

blocks.text: Use Strickland:

The deficiency caused prejudice
Prejudice usually requires a reasonable probability that the result would have been different.

sections.title: 116. Presumed Prejudice
body: Prejudice may be presumed when:
Counsel is completely denied at a critical stage
Counsel entirely fails to subject the prosecution’s case to meaningful adversarial testing
An actual conflict adversely affects representation
Government interference makes effective assistance impossible

blocks.text: Prejudice may be presumed when:

sections.title: 117. Conflicts of Interest
body: A defendant must usually show:
Actual conflict
Adverse effect on counsel’s performance
When the court improperly refuses to investigate a timely conflict objection, reversal may be automatic.

blocks.text: A defendant must usually show:

Adverse effect on counsel’s performance

sections.title: 118. Plea Bargaining and Counsel
body: Counsel must communicate formal plea offers.
To prove prejudice from a lost offer, the defendant generally must show a reasonable probability that:
The defendant would have accepted
The prosecution would not have withdrawn
The court would have accepted
The outcome would have been more favorable

sections.title: 119. Identification Procedures Overview
body: PART IV — PRETRIAL PROCEDURE
Identification may be:
Corporeal — lineup or show-up
Noncorporeal — photo array
Two separate constitutional rules may apply:
Sixth Amendment right to counsel
Due process protection against suggestiveness

blocks.text: PART IV — PRETRIAL PROCEDURE
Identification may be:

Noncorporeal — photo array
Two separate constitutional rules may apply:
Sixth Amendment right to counsel
Due process protection against suggestiveness

sections.title: 120. Counsel at Identifications
body: The defendant has a right to counsel at a postindictment lineup or show-up.
There is no Sixth Amendment right to counsel at:
Preindictment lineup
Photo array, whether before or after indictment

blocks.text: The defendant has a right to counsel at a postindictment lineup or show-up.

Photo array, whether before or after indictment

sections.title: 121. Remedy for Lineup Counsel Violation
body: Evidence of the unconstitutional lineup identification is excluded.
An in-court identification may still be admitted if the prosecution proves an independent basis.

blocks.text: Evidence of the unconstitutional lineup identification is excluded.
An in-court identification may still be admitted if the prosecution proves an independent basis.

sections.title: 122. Suggestive Identification Test
body: A police-arranged identification violates due process when:
The procedure was unnecessarily suggestive
There was a substantial likelihood of misidentification
Reliability may save the identification.

blocks.text: A police-arranged identification violates due process when:

There was a substantial likelihood of misidentification
Reliability may save the identification.

sections.title: 123. Identification Reliability Factors
body: Consider:
Opportunity to view
Degree of attention
Accuracy of prior description
Certainty at identification
Time between crime and identification
Memory line: View, attention, description, certainty, time.

blocks.text: Consider:
Opportunity to view
Degree of attention
Accuracy of prior description
Certainty at identification
Time between crime and identification

sections.title: 124. Preliminary Hearing
body: A preliminary hearing determines whether probable cause supports continued prosecution.
The defendant generally has a right to counsel.
The hearing is not constitutionally required after a grand jury indictment.

blocks.text: A preliminary hearing determines whether probable cause supports continued prosecution.
The defendant generally has a right to counsel.
The hearing is not constitutionally required after a grand jury indictment.

sections.title: 125. Bail
body: The Eighth Amendment prohibits excessive bail.
There is no absolute constitutional right to bail.
Preventive detention may be allowed when supported by a legitimate regulatory purpose and proper procedures.

blocks.text: The Eighth Amendment prohibits excessive bail.
There is no absolute constitutional right to bail.
Preventive detention may be allowed when supported by a legitimate regulatory purpose and proper procedures.

sections.title: 126. Competency
body: A defendant is incompetent when unable to:
Understand the proceedings
Consult rationally with counsel
Assist in the defense
The court must order evaluation when substantial doubt arises.
An incompetent defendant may not be tried or plead guilty.

blocks.text: A defendant is incompetent when unable to:

Consult rationally with counsel
Assist in the defense
The court must order evaluation when substantial doubt arises.
An incompetent defendant may not be tried or plead guilty.

sections.title: 127. Grand Jury
body: The federal government generally must use a grand jury for serious federal crimes.
The grand-jury requirement is not incorporated against the states.
Grand jury proceedings are secret and usually one-sided.

blocks.text: The federal government generally must use a grand jury for serious federal crimes.
The grand-jury requirement is not incorporated against the states.
Grand jury proceedings are secret and usually one-sided.

sections.title: 128. Grand Jury Rights
body: A grand jury witness generally has:
No right to have counsel inside the room
No Miranda right merely because of testimony
The Fifth Amendment privilege against incriminating answers
An indictment may rest on hearsay or evidence inadmissible at trial.

blocks.text: A grand jury witness generally has:

No Miranda right merely because of testimony
The Fifth Amendment privilege against incriminating answers
An indictment may rest on hearsay or evidence inadmissible at trial.

sections.title: 129. Discrimination in Grand Jury Selection
body: Intentional racial discrimination in selecting grand jurors violates equal protection.
A conviction based on an indictment from a discriminatorily selected grand jury must be reversed.

blocks.text: Intentional racial discrimination in selecting grand jurors violates equal protection.
A conviction based on an indictment from a discriminatorily selected grand jury must be reversed.

sections.title: 130. Brady Disclosure
body: The prosecution must disclose material evidence favorable to the defense.
The defendant must show:
Evidence was favorable because it was exculpatory or impeaching
The evidence was suppressed
There is a reasonable probability of a different outcome
Intentional misconduct is unnecessary.

blocks.text: The prosecution must disclose material evidence favorable to the defense.

There is a reasonable probability of a different outcome
Intentional misconduct is unnecessary.

sections.title: 131. Right to Be Present
body: PART V — TRIAL RIGHTS
A defendant generally has the right to be present at:
Arraignment
Jury selection
Trial
Verdict
Sentencing
The right may be lost through voluntary absence or seriously disruptive behavior after warning.

Trial
Verdict
Sentencing
The right may be lost through voluntary absence or seriously disruptive behavior after warning.

sections.title: 132. Right to Jury Trial
body: The Sixth Amendment requires a jury for serious offenses.
An offense is generally serious when the authorized imprisonment exceeds six months.
Multiple petty offenses are not aggregated merely to create a jury right.

blocks.text: The Sixth Amendment requires a jury for serious offenses.
An offense is generally serious when the authorized imprisonment exceeds six months.
Multiple petty offenses are not aggregated merely to create a jury right.

sections.title: 133. Jury Size and Unanimity
body: A criminal jury must have at least six members.
Both state and federal criminal juries must return unanimous guilty verdicts.
Federal criminal juries ordinarily have 12 members unless a valid rule permits fewer.

blocks.text: A criminal jury must have at least six members.
Both state and federal criminal juries must return unanimous guilty verdicts.
Federal criminal juries ordinarily have 12 members unless a valid rule permits fewer.

sections.title: 134. Fair Cross Section
body: The jury venire must represent a fair cross section of the community.
The defendant must show:
A distinctive group
Unfair underrepresentation
Systematic exclusion
The seated jury itself need not mirror community demographics.

blocks.text: The jury venire must represent a fair cross section of the community.
The defendant must show:
A distinctive group
Unfair underrepresentation
Systematic exclusion
The seated jury itself need not mirror community demographics.

sections.title: 135. Batson Challenges
body: Peremptory strikes may not be based on race or sex.
The process:
Prima facie showing of discrimination
Striking party gives a neutral explanation
Court determines purposeful discrimination
The explanation need not be persuasive at step two, but it must be genuine and neutral.

blocks.text: Peremptory strikes may not be based on race or sex.
The process:

Striking party gives a neutral explanation
Court determines purposeful discrimination
The explanation need not be persuasive at step two, but it must be genuine and neutral.

sections.title: 136. Death-Qualified Juries
body: Jurors may be excluded for cause when their views would substantially impair their duties.
A juror cannot be removed merely for expressing general reservations about capital punishment.

blocks.text: Jurors may be excluded for cause when their views would substantially impair their duties.
A juror cannot be removed merely for expressing general reservations about capital punishment.

sections.title: 137. Guilty Pleas
body: A guilty plea must be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary.
The defendant must understand:
Nature of the charge
Essential consequences
Maximum and minimum penalties
Rights waived
Federal procedure also requires a factual basis.

blocks.text: A guilty plea must be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary.
The defendant must understand:
Nature of the charge
Essential consequences
Maximum and minimum penalties
Rights waived
Federal procedure also requires a factual basis.

sections.title: 138. Rights Waived by Plea
body: A valid guilty plea generally waives:
Jury trial
Confrontation
Self-incrimination at trial
Most nonjurisdictional preplea defects
A plea may still be challenged for:
Involuntariness
Ineffective assistance
Lack of jurisdiction
Certain double-jeopardy claims

Most nonjurisdictional preplea defects

Ineffective assistance

sections.title: 139. Speedy Trial
body: The Sixth Amendment speedy-trial right begins at arrest or formal accusation, whichever occurs first.
Use LARP:
Length of delay
Assigned reason
Right asserted
Prejudice
The remedy is dismissal with prejudice.

blocks.text: The Sixth Amendment speedy-trial right begins at arrest or formal accusation, whichever occurs first.
Use LARP:
Length of delay
Assigned reason
Right asserted
Prejudice
The remedy is dismissal with prejudice.

sections.title: 140. Preaccusation Delay
body: Before formal accusation, statutes of limitations are the primary protection.
Due process may be violated when delay:
Causes actual prejudice
Was intentionally used to gain tactical advantage or harass
Good-faith investigation generally does not violate due process.

blocks.text: Before formal accusation, statutes of limitations are the primary protection.
Due process may be violated when delay:
Causes actual prejudice
Was intentionally used to gain tactical advantage or harass
Good-faith investigation generally does not violate due process.

sections.title: 141. Public Trial
body: The defendant has a right to a public trial, including many pretrial proceedings.
Closure requires:
Overriding interest
Narrow tailoring
Consideration of alternatives
Adequate findings

blocks.text: The defendant has a right to a public trial, including many pretrial proceedings.
Closure requires:
Overriding interest
Narrow tailoring
Consideration of alternatives
Adequate findings

sections.title: 142. Fair-Trial Publicity
body: A court may protect fairness through:
Change of venue
Continuance
Searching voir dire
Sequestration
Instructions
Restrictive orders directed at participants
A prior restraint on the press is extremely difficult to justify.

blocks.text: A court may protect fairness through:
Change of venue
Continuance
Searching voir dire
Sequestration
Instructions
Restrictive orders directed at participants
A prior restraint on the press is extremely difficult to justify.

sections.title: 143. Confrontation Clause
body: In a criminal case, testimonial hearsay from an unavailable declarant is generally inadmissible unless the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine.
The rule applies only to testimonial statements offered against the accused.

blocks.text: In a criminal case, testimonial hearsay from an unavailable declarant is generally inadmissible unless the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine.
The rule applies only to testimonial statements offered against the accused.

sections.title: 144. Testimonial Statements
body: Usually testimonial:
Formal police interrogation about past events
Affidavits
Forensic certificates prepared for prosecution
Prior testimony
Formal accusations
Usually nontestimonial:
Statements made to resolve an ongoing emergency
Casual remarks
Most statements to private individuals
Business records created for ordinary operations

Affidavits
Forensic certificates prepared for prosecution
Prior testimony
Formal accusations

Casual remarks
Most statements to private individuals

sections.title: 145. Ongoing Emergency
body: Statements made primarily to obtain help during an ongoing emergency are generally nontestimonial.
Once the emergency ends and questioning shifts to reconstructing past events, later statements may become testimonial.

blocks.text: Statements made primarily to obtain help during an ongoing emergency are generally nontestimonial.
Once the emergency ends and questioning shifts to reconstructing past events, later statements may become testimonial.

sections.title: 146. Forfeiture by Wrongdoing
body: A defendant forfeits confrontation rights when the defendant wrongfully causes the witness’s unavailability with the purpose of preventing testimony.
Mere causation is not enough; intent to silence is required.

blocks.text: A defendant forfeits confrontation rights when the defendant wrongfully causes the witness’s unavailability with the purpose of preventing testimony.
Mere causation is not enough; intent to silence is required.

sections.title: 147. Bruton Rule
body: At a joint jury trial, admitting a nontestifying codefendant’s confession that directly incriminates the defendant violates confrontation.
A limiting instruction does not cure the violation.
Possible solutions:
Severance
Exclusion
Codefendant testimony
Proper neutral redaction

Codefendant testimony
Proper neutral redaction

sections.title: 148. Neutral Redactions
body: Replacing the defendant’s name with an obvious blank, deletion, or symbol that directly identifies the defendant remains unconstitutional.
A genuinely neutral reference that does not directly identify the defendant may be admitted with a limiting instruction.

blocks.text: Replacing the defendant’s name with an obvious blank, deletion, or symbol that directly identifies the defendant remains unconstitutional.
A genuinely neutral reference that does not directly identify the defendant may be admitted with a limiting instruction.

sections.title: 149. Presumption of Innocence and Burden
body: The prosecution must prove every element beyond a reasonable doubt.
The defendant is presumed innocent.
A mandatory presumption that shifts the burden of disproving an element to the defendant is unconstitutional.

blocks.text: The prosecution must prove every element beyond a reasonable doubt.
The defendant is presumed innocent.
A mandatory presumption that shifts the burden of disproving an element to the defendant is unconstitutional.

sections.title: 150. Permissive Inferences
body: A jury may be allowed, but not required, to infer an elemental fact from proof of another fact when the connection is rational.
Permissive language is safer than mandatory language.

blocks.text: A jury may be allowed, but not required, to infer an elemental fact from proof of another fact when the connection is rational.
Permissive language is safer than mandatory language.

sections.title: 151. Right to Testify and Present a Defense
body: A defendant has a constitutional right to:
Testify
Call witnesses
Present relevant evidence
Obtain compulsory process
Reasonable evidentiary restrictions remain valid.

blocks.text: A defendant has a constitutional right to:
Testify
Call witnesses
Present relevant evidence
Obtain compulsory process
Reasonable evidentiary restrictions remain valid.

sections.title: 152. Sentencing and Apprendi
body: Except for the fact of a prior conviction, any fact increasing the statutory maximum punishment must be:
Charged as required
Submitted to a jury
Proved beyond a reasonable doubt
A judge may generally decide whether multiple sentences run concurrently or consecutively.

blocks.text: Except for the fact of a prior conviction, any fact increasing the statutory maximum punishment must be:
Charged as required
Submitted to a jury
Proved beyond a reasonable doubt
A judge may generally decide whether multiple sentences run concurrently or consecutively.

sections.title: 153. Right to Counsel at Sentencing
body: Sentencing is a critical stage.
Counsel is also required at a revocation proceeding when a previously deferred sentence may be imposed.

blocks.text: Sentencing is a critical stage.
Counsel is also required at a revocation proceeding when a previously deferred sentence may be imposed.

sections.title: 154. Eighth Amendment Overview
body: The Eighth Amendment prohibits:
Excessive bail
Excessive fines
Cruel and unusual punishment
It applies primarily to punishment after conviction.

blocks.text: The Eighth Amendment prohibits:
Excessive bail
Excessive fines
Cruel and unusual punishment
It applies primarily to punishment after conviction.

sections.title: 155. Proportionality
body: A noncapital sentence violates the Eighth Amendment only when grossly disproportionate.
Courts give legislatures substantial deference.
Capital punishment receives much stricter proportionality review.

blocks.text: A noncapital sentence violates the Eighth Amendment only when grossly disproportionate.
Courts give legislatures substantial deference.
Capital punishment receives much stricter proportionality review.

sections.title: 156. Prison Conditions
body: Prison conditions violate the Eighth Amendment when officials are deliberately indifferent to a substantial risk of serious harm.
Negligence alone is insufficient.

blocks.text: Prison conditions violate the Eighth Amendment when officials are deliberately indifferent to a substantial risk of serious harm.
Negligence alone is insufficient.

sections.title: 157. Excessive Force in Prison
body: Force violates the Eighth Amendment when applied maliciously and sadistically to cause harm rather than in a good-faith effort to maintain discipline.
Serious injury is relevant but not always required.

blocks.text: Force violates the Eighth Amendment when applied maliciously and sadistically to cause harm rather than in a good-faith effort to maintain discipline.
Serious injury is relevant but not always required.

sections.title: 158. Medical Care in Prison
body: Prison officials violate the Eighth Amendment through deliberate indifference to serious medical needs.
Medical negligence or malpractice alone is not enough.

blocks.text: Prison officials violate the Eighth Amendment through deliberate indifference to serious medical needs.
Medical negligence or malpractice alone is not enough.

sections.title: 159. Double Jeopardy Protections
body: PART VI — POST-TRIAL
The Double Jeopardy Clause protects against:
Retrial after acquittal
Retrial after conviction
Multiple punishments for the same offense

blocks.text: PART VI — POST-TRIAL
The Double Jeopardy Clause protects against:
Retrial after acquittal
Retrial after conviction
Multiple punishments for the same offense

sections.title: 160. Blockburger Test
body: Two offenses are different only if each requires proof of an element the other does not.
A lesser included offense is the same offense as the greater offense for double-jeopardy purposes.

blocks.text: Two offenses are different only if each requires proof of an element the other does not.
A lesser included offense is the same offense as the greater offense for double-jeopardy purposes.

sections.title: 161. Greater and Lesser Offenses
body: Successive prosecution for a greater and lesser included offense is generally barred.
An exception exists when a necessary fact for the greater offense occurs only after the first trial.
Example: A victim dies after the defendant has already been convicted of battery. A later homicide prosecution may proceed.

blocks.text: Successive prosecution for a greater and lesser included offense is generally barred.
An exception exists when a necessary fact for the greater offense occurs only after the first trial.
Example: A victim dies after the defendant has already been convicted of battery. A later homicide prosecution may proceed.

sections.title: 162. Legislative Authorization
body: At one trial, cumulative punishment for overlapping statutory offenses may be imposed when the legislature clearly authorizes it.
Double jeopardy primarily protects against punishment beyond legislative intent.

blocks.text: At one trial, cumulative punishment for overlapping statutory offenses may be imposed when the legislature clearly authorizes it.
Double jeopardy primarily protects against punishment beyond legislative intent.

sections.title: 163. Conspiracy and the Substantive Crime
body: Conspiracy and the completed substantive offense are different offenses because each requires an element the other does not.
They may generally be separately prosecuted and punished.

blocks.text: Conspiracy and the completed substantive offense are different offenses because each requires an element the other does not.
They may generally be separately prosecuted and punished.

sections.title: 164. Attachment of Jeopardy
body: Jeopardy attaches:
Jury trial — when the jury is empaneled and sworn
Bench trial — when the first witness is sworn
Guilty plea — generally when the court accepts the plea

blocks.text: Jeopardy attaches:

Bench trial — when the first witness is sworn
Guilty plea — generally when the court accepts the plea

sections.title: 165. Acquittals
body: A final acquittal bars retrial even if based on:
Legal error
Factual error
Misinterpretation of the law
The government generally cannot appeal a true acquittal.

blocks.text: A final acquittal bars retrial even if based on:

Factual error
Misinterpretation of the law
The government generally cannot appeal a true acquittal.

sections.title: 166. Dual Sovereignty
body: Different sovereigns may prosecute the same conduct under their own laws.
Examples:
State and federal government
Two different states
A state and its municipality are ordinarily the same sovereign.

blocks.text: Different sovereigns may prosecute the same conduct under their own laws.

Two different states
A state and its municipality are ordinarily the same sovereign.

sections.title: 167. Mistrials
body: Retrial after mistrial is permitted when:
The defendant requests or consents, unless provoked by prosecutorial bad faith
Manifest necessity exists, such as a hung jury or juror illness
A prosecution-requested mistrial caused merely by lack of preparation may bar retrial.

blocks.text: Retrial after mistrial is permitted when:

Manifest necessity exists, such as a hung jury or juror illness
A prosecution-requested mistrial caused merely by lack of preparation may bar retrial.

sections.title: 168. Retrial After Appeal
body: When a conviction is reversed for trial error, retrial generally is permitted.
When reversed for legally insufficient evidence, retrial is barred because insufficiency is equivalent to acquittal.
A defendant may receive a harsher sentence after retrial only when justified by objective events occurring after the original sentence.

blocks.text: When a conviction is reversed for trial error, retrial generally is permitted.
